unit.name: From CLIP to multimodal transformers and FLAVA
unit.description: Covers contrastive vision-language learning, BERT-era multimodal transformers, and the move toward unified multimodal foundation models.
unit.summary: Late fusion is reframed as modern contrastive learning, with CLIP as the canonical example: independently encode text and images, use in-batch negatives, and rank matched pairs above mismatched ones [ts=1402s][ts=1441s]. Kiela emphasizes that CLIP’s leap came less from a novel loss than from transformer encoders and massive web-scale paired data, enabling prompt-based zero-shot classification and robust transfer; ALIGN and LAION continue the same scaling story with more data [ts=1503s][ts=1561s][ts=1667s]. He then walks through BERT-era multimodal transformers such as VisualBERT, ViLBERT, MMBT, PixelBERT, UNITER, and ViLT, contrasting single-stream versus dual-stream fusion and showing the trend from detector features toward raw patch-based transformer inputs [ts=1828s][ts=1869s][ts=2021s][ts=2061s]. The segment culminates in FLAVA, a “one model to rule them all” design that jointly pretrains image-only, text-only, and multimodal objectives, combining masked image modeling, masked language modeling, image-text matching, and a global contrastive loss in one shared foundation model [ts=2207s][ts=2331s][ts=2399s].

key_points.text: CLIP trains text and image encoders independently and uses an in-batch contrastive ranking objective over aligned image-text pairs.
Prompting with templates like “a photo of a ...” enables zero-shot image classification with CLIP-style models.
Scaling data and compute, as in ALIGN and LAION, improves contrastive vision-language models even when the core idea stays the same.
VisualBERT is a single-stream model that concatenates visual and textual inputs into one transformer, while ViLBERT uses two streams with cross-attention.
ViLT removes detector-style preprocessing and feeds image patches directly into the transformer.
When controlled for data and training setup, many multimodal BERT variants are nearly equivalent, implying data matters more than minor architectural tweaks.
FLAVA jointly trains unimodal and multimodal objectives so one model can handle vision, language, and their combination.

unit_summary_for_generation: Late fusion is reframed as modern contrastive learning, with CLIP as the canonical example: independently encode text and images, use in-batch negatives, and rank matched pairs above mismatched ones [ts=1402s][ts=1441s]. Kiela emphasizes that CLIP’s leap came less from a novel loss than from transformer encoders and massive web-scale paired data, enabling prompt-based zero-shot classification and robust transfer; ALIGN and LAION continue the same scaling story with more data [ts=1503s][ts=1561s][ts=1667s]. He then walks through BERT-era multimodal transformers such as VisualBERT, ViLBERT, MMBT, PixelBERT, UNITER, and ViLT, contrasting single-stream versus dual-stream fusion and showing the trend from detector features toward raw patch-based transformer inputs [ts=1828s][ts=1869s][ts=2021s][ts=2061s]. The segment culminates in FLAVA, a “one model to rule them all” design that jointly pretrains image-only, text-only, and multimodal objectives, combining masked image modeling, masked language modeling, image-text matching, and a global contrastive loss in one shared foundation model [ts=2207s][ts=2331s][ts=2399s].

kp_catalog.name: Contrastive vision-language pretraining
kp_catalog.description: The CLIP/ALIGN family of models that align independently encoded images and text with contrastive objectives and scale primarily through data and compute. CLIP trains an image encoder and text encoder so matching image-text pairs are close and mismatched pairs are far in a shared embedding space.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because contrastive vision-language pretraining formalizes how the CLIP/ALIGN family of models that align independently encoded images and text with contrastive objectives and scale primarily through data and compute, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Multimodal transformer pretraining
kp_catalog.description: Single-stream and dual-stream transformer architectures that combine image and text tokens, often with masked prediction and image-text matching objectives.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because multimodal transformer pretraining clarifies single-stream and dual-stream transformer architectures that combine image and text tokens, often with masked prediction and image-text matching objectives, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Unified multimodal foundation models
kp_catalog.description: The idea that one jointly pretrained model should handle unimodal and multimodal tasks rather than requiring separate systems.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because unified multimodal foundation models synthesizes the idea that one jointly pretrained model should handle unimodal and multimodal tasks rather than requiring separate systems into an end-to-end method or integrated recipe.
